Next Action: Engage startup counsel to file certificate of incorporation
Notes: Delaware preferred over Florida for future fundraising. Most VCs require or strongly prefer Delaware C-Corps. Florida LLC conversion later is costly and creates tax events.

Next Action: File Application by Foreign Corporation for Authorization to Transact Business in Florida after Delaware formation
Notes: Required since operations are based in Florida. Annual report due by May 1 each year. Filing fee $70 plus $35 registered agent designation.

Next Action: Engage registered agent service or use counsel
Notes: Required for foreign qualification in Florida. Use commercial service (Northwest Registered Agent or similar) rather than founder's home address for privacy.

Next Action: Apply via IRS Form SS-4 online after Delaware formation
Notes: Required for banking opening payroll vendor accounts. Free direct from IRS — do not pay third-party services.

Next Action: Choose bank after EIN received
Notes: Mercury or Brex preferred for startups. Avoid traditional banks for fewer fees and better integrations. Need EIN and formation docs.
Open Questions: Are personal and business activities properly separated for liability purposes?

Next Action: Have startup counsel draft and execute SPA with proper vesting
Notes: Standard 4-year vest with 1-year cliff. Must be done at formation when stock value is nominal. Failure to do this properly creates massive tax problems later.

Next Action: File within 30 days of stock issuance via certified mail to IRS
Notes: HARD DEADLINE — 30 days from stock purchase date. No extensions. Failure to file creates ordinary income tax on vesting gains rather than capital gains treatment. Send certified mail with return receipt.

Next Action: Have counsel draft PIIA covering all pre-incorporation work
Notes: Confidential Information and Invention Assignment Agreement. Must cover everything created before formation. Single largest cause of fundraising delays and acquisition blowups.

Next Action: Identify every contractor or freelancer who touched code/design/copy and execute IP assignment
Notes: Anyone who contributed to product without proper assignment creates a cloud on title. Includes the developer who built the prototype. No exceptions — get signatures or rebuild that work.

Next Action: Map every file/module in the repo to documented assignment chain back to company
Notes: Operational verification of the contractor IP assignment work. For each contributor identified produce a written record showing what they touched and that an assignment covers it. Required artifact for any future diligence.

Next Action: Run license scan on codebase and document all OSS dependencies and their licenses
Notes: GPL/AGPL contamination is an acquisition killer for software companies. Free tools (FOSSA Free Tier GitHub dependency graph) suffice initially. Document the result — needed in diligence regardless of findings.
Open Questions: Is there GPL or AGPL contamination in the current codebase?

Next Action: Create company GitHub org and transfer all repos from personal account
Notes: Repos under company organization not founder personal account. Configure branch protection 2FA enforcement and audit log. Common early mistake to leave critical IP under personal account.

Next Action: Engage TM counsel for full knockout and clearance search on company name and product name
Notes: Florida common-law marks plus federal USPTO search. Comprehensive search costs $500-1500. Skipping this creates rebrand risk after launch.
Open Questions: Will the planned product name survive trademark scrutiny?

Next Action: File ITU application after clearance search confirms availability
Notes: Class 9 (software) and Class 42 (SaaS) most common for software startups. Filing fee $250-350 per class. ITU allows filing before commercial use.

Next Action: Verify all critical TLDs and handles locked before any public mention
Notes: .com is mandatory. Acquire variations and common misspellings. Lock handles on X LinkedIn Instagram even if not used. Defensive only.

Next Action: Set up Workspace on primary domain with founder as super admin
Notes: Business Standard tier. Configure proper admin separation — founder personal account separate from admin account. Enable 2FA mandatory for all users.

Next Action: Catalog every login owner backup-access for all critical services
Notes: Single largest source of operational fragility in early startups. Use 1Password or Bitwarden business tier. Founder cannot be single point of failure.

Next Action: Centralize DNS at Cloudflare with founder as primary owner and one backup
Notes: DNS hijack or lockout is catastrophic. Cloudflare offers free tier and DNSSEC. Never let a contractor or vendor hold DNS.

Next Action: Counsel drafts employment agreement covering duties compensation IP termination
Notes: Separate from Stock Purchase Agreement. Defines role compensation and termination terms. Standard at formation. Investors will ask for it in diligence.

Next Action: Counsel drafts mutual NDA customer NDA and vendor NDA templates
Notes: Reusable templates needed before any meaningful external conversation. One-time legal spend creates ongoing leverage. Mutual NDA is the most-used.

Next Action: Interview 2-3 startup-focused CPAs
Notes: Florida has no state income tax but Delaware franchise tax applies. R&D tax credit eligibility matters. Avoid generalist accountants — need startup experience.

Next Action: Quote general liability and cyber liability
Notes: Vouch or Embroker for startup-specific policies. Cyber liability becomes mandatory before enterprise sales. General liability often required by office lease or partner contracts.

Next Action: Set up Carta or Pulley before any stock issuance beyond founders
Notes: Free tier exists for early stage. Critical before issuing options or taking investment. Manual cap tables in spreadsheets cause material errors.

Initiative: Draft Customer Terms of Service and Privacy Policy
Next Action: Have counsel draft TOS and Privacy Policy compliant with Florida and federal requirements
Notes: Florida Digital Bill of Rights effective July 2024 — applies to controllers processing data on 100k+ Florida consumers. Get counsel-drafted versions not templates.
Open Questions: Does the product create AI output liability exposure?,Does the Florida Digital Bill of Rights apply to the company?

Next Action: Draft one-page document on grant level bands refresh policy and acceleration terms
Notes: Establishes consistent approach before first hire. Prevents inconsistent grants which create internal conflict later. One page is enough — not a formal policy.

Initiative: Obtain Spousal Consent on Founder Stock
Next Action: Have founder spouse (if applicable) sign acknowledgment regarding stock ownership
Notes: Florida is not a community property state so lower priority than CA. Still useful as cheap insurance against future divorce diligence question. One-time signed acknowledgment.

Next Action: Document which AI providers product depends on and contingency if pricing/access changes
Notes: Material risk for AI-native products. Identify primary provider and at least one fallback. Estimate switching cost. Required diligence artifact for any AI-first company.

Next Action: Adopt equity incentive plan with board approval
Notes: Typical 10-15% option pool reserved. Required before hiring with equity. 409A valuation needed before granting options — defer until first hire planned.
Deferred Rationale: Premature until first equity hire is identified

Initiative: Set Up Florida Sales Tax Registration
Next Action: Register with Florida Department of Revenue if selling taxable services
Notes: SaaS taxability in Florida is nuanced — generally non-taxable but data processing services may be taxable. CPA should advise based on product specifics.
Deferred Rationale: Defer until revenue model is confirmed and customers identified
Open Questions: Does Florida sales tax apply to the planned revenue model?

Next Action: No action required yet
Notes: Required only when enterprise customers demand it. Premature spend before enterprise pipeline exists. Vanta or Drata when needed — 4-6 months to Type I.
Deferred Rationale: Premature before enterprise sales pipeline exists

Next Action: Evaluate after technical differentiation is validated by market
Notes: Provisional gives 12 months to file full application. $300 USPTO fee plus counsel time. Only valuable if there is genuinely novel technical IP — most software is not patentable in practice post-Alice.
Deferred Rationale: Awaiting validation of differentiated technical workflow
